title: The future belongs to AI maniacs - Marginal REVOLUTION
gist: Tyler Cowen profiles 'AI maniacs' -- young, credential-skeptical, obsessive early adopters who use AI agents to run their lives and build small, AI-monitored companies -- and argues immigrants are disproportionately likely to be among them, pointing to Stripe data showing single-person companies earning $10M+ have doubled in two years.

insights.claim: Cowen defines an 'AI maniac' as someone obsessed with working with the latest AI models: trying new models as soon as they're available, spending hours mastering them, and using them to regulate both their workflows and their personal lives.
insights.speaker: Tyler Cowen
insights.so_what: Defines a distinct, identifiable user segment (extreme early adopters) worth tracking as a leading indicator for AI product adoption and demand.
insights.locator: An AI maniac is someone who is obsessed

insights.claim: Cowen describes one person he knows who has his AI agent text him if he isn't drinking enough water, using cameras placed around his house to monitor the behavior.
insights.speaker: Tyler Cowen
insights.so_what: Illustrates real consumer demand for pervasive, personal-life AI agent monitoring -- a signal for personal-AI/quantified-self product opportunities.
insights.locator: I know one person who has his AI agent text him

insights.claim: An online anecdote describes a man canceling a date to spend more time using 'Claude Fable 5' after Anthropic extended the model's availability for a few days; Cowen discloses he sits on Anthropic's economic advisory board.
insights.speaker: Tyler Cowen
insights.so_what: An extreme but telling illustration of engagement intensity some users have with frontier AI models, relevant to engagement/retention metrics for model providers.
insights.locator: One online anecdote tells of a man

insights.claim: Many AI maniacs use AI tools to start smaller, cheaper companies than were previously possible, with a single human setting in motion and monitoring a large number of AI tools and agents, then reaping outsize profits as the company grows.
insights.speaker: Tyler Cowen
insights.so_what: Points to a structural shift toward ultra-lean, AI-orchestrated company formation, favoring tooling that helps a single founder supervise many AI agents.
insights.locator: Many AI maniacs are using AI tools to start companies

insights.claim: Stripe customer data shows the number of single-person companies earning $10 million or more in revenue has doubled over the past two years; Cowen says there's no firm estimate of how much of that is due to AI but believes it stands to reason AI is a main driver.
insights.speaker: Tyler Cowen
insights.so_what: A concrete, if imprecise, data point quantifying the AI-driven productivity leverage available to solo founders -- relevant for VCs evaluating lean, high-revenue-per-employee startups.
insights.locator: Stripe, the payments company, recently issued customer data

insights.claim: AI maniacs tend to be young, often lack conventional manners and social graces because they want to 'get right to it,' and are unusually able to imagine a future very different from the present.
insights.speaker: Tyler Cowen
insights.so_what: Suggests demographic and psychographic targeting cues for products aimed at heavy AI adopters.
insights.locator: Anecdotally, I observe that AI maniacs tend to be young

insights.claim: Cowen argues many AI maniacs are motivated by kindness, seeing the potential for new AI services in areas like biomedicine to help other people, which he views as offsetting their obsessiveness.
insights.speaker: Tyler Cowen
insights.so_what: Frames AI-maniac motivation as partly altruistic/mission-driven, relevant to hiring and community-building for AI-focused ventures, especially in biomedicine.
insights.locator: Many of them also are kind

insights.claim: AI maniacs are skeptical of credentials, and Cowen agrees they're right to be, arguing that Harvard and Yale are not where you learn to effectively use AI tools -- you must self-teach, with help from other AI maniacs and from the AI tools themselves.
insights.speaker: Tyler Cowen
insights.so_what: A contrarian signal that elite academic credentials are losing relevance as a gatekeeper for cutting-edge AI skill, favoring self-taught/community-trained talent pools for hiring.
insights.locator: The AI maniacs also are skeptical of credentials

insights.claim: The few AI maniacs found in the Ivy League tend to be those who have instead invested their energy into other, more traditionally established paths to success, implying elite credentialing selects against AI-maniac behavior rather than for it.
insights.speaker: Tyler Cowen
insights.so_what: Suggests recruiting the most intense AI talent may require looking outside traditional elite-university pipelines.
insights.locator: There are some AI maniacs in the Ivy League

insights.claim: Cowen believes immigrants are especially likely to be AI maniacs because they have fewer traditional channels to rise through credentials, family connections, and establishment modes of thinking, making them more willing to try something new, and because switching countries selects for people with higher energy, courage, and ambition.
insights.speaker: Tyler Cowen
insights.so_what: A specific, testable demographic prediction linking immigration status to AI-driven entrepreneurship, relevant to immigration-policy debates around high-skill visas and startup formation.
insights.locator: I also believe that immigrants are especially likely
